Alex Jones DID NOT predict 9/11 with any degree of accuracy.

Nothing he said proves there was a conspiracy! He delivered a rambling monologue that simply threw out any semi-plausible possibility to cover virtually every base. 

Yes, he mentioned bin Laden, plane explosions, and the Twin Towers, but he also mentioned nuclear attack and biological attacks, and said further that such an attack “would happen in Europe, Asia, or Latin America, or the United States.” Even the attributes that did feature in some way on 9/11 were not prescient or inside information of any sort!

The fact that he mentioned Osama bin Laden proves nothing but that Jones keeps up with the news, as bin Laden was already well known, having attacked US embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in 1998, the USS Cole in Yemen in 2000, and gone on a media blitz with Western outlets warning them of violence if America did not stop its unjust and deleterious actions and involvement in the Middle East. 

Further undermining Jones, al-Qaeda was also in the news that year, as the federal trial for four of the bombers involved in the 1998 attacks began in early 2001 and stretched on for months. Jones was not revealing any new or secret information, but rather exploiting the existing tensions and threats that were already public knowledge.

The fact that Jones mentioned planes blowing up is not evidence of a conspiracy. It was not even such that it could be labeled a prediction, but rather playing to a common fear. 

The FAA had been constantly under guard since the earliest days of aviation, long before the War on Terror. Even the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland in 1988 by Libyan agents underscored why anyone would have still recognized the possibility of a plane attack. Jones was not foreseeing anything, but rather playing on the existing anxiety and vulnerability of air travel.
The fact that Jones mentioned the WTC was not a proof, or even a coincidence. The WTC was a known target for its size and illustrious image as a symbol of American capitalism and power. Indeed, that is why Osama bin Laden chose to attack the Twin Towers specifically, as he and al-Qaeda repeatedly stated and explained. stated in his video confession after 9/11. 

Jones was not identifying or predicting anything, but rather picking an obvious and prominent landmark that had already been attacked before.

Jones has a history of making false and sensational predictions that never pan out including: the end of the world in 2012, the invasion of Texas by the US military in 2015, and the assassination of Donald Trump in 2016.

The claim that Alex Jones predicted 9/11 in detail is a blatant and misleading falsehood.

comment_content:
I was six years old when the world broke open.

That morning, my dad wore his favorite blue tie — the one he called his “power tie” — and my mom made me cinnamon toast, even though we were running late. She always made mornings feel warm. Safe. Ordinary.

Dad kissed the top of my head and said, “Be good today, champ.” I didn’t know that would be the last thing he ever said to me.

He worked in the North Tower.

Mom dropped me off at school with a smile that didn’t reach her eyes — she had that nurse’s intuition, that quiet sense that something was coming. Before I walked through the school doors, she pulled me back into a hug. Long. Tight. The kind of hug you never forget.

She went to the hospital right after — just a few blocks from the towers.

I remember the sirens first. Then the way the teachers’ faces changed when the news came in. Some kids were pulled out of class in a rush. Others cried. I waited, small and confused, clutching my Spider-Man lunchbox.

No one came for me.

Hours passed. And then days. People spoke in hushed tones. "We can't reach them." "Still missing." "Maybe they got out."

But they didn’t.

For years, I lived in the in-between — between the child I had been and the weight of a world that had crumbled. I moved in with my aunt, who tried her best to hold together what was left of our little family. She taught me to pray. She held me when the nightmares came.

Every year on September 11th, I visited the memorial. I traced their names with my fingers — Miguel and Lucia — and tried to remember the way their laughter sounded in our old apartment. Sometimes I could. Sometimes I couldn’t.

Grief doesn’t go away. It just grows quieter. Becomes part of your shadow.

But here’s the thing: I didn’t break. I grew.

I kept their photos beside my bed. I studied hard. I lived for both of them — not just to make them proud, but to prove that love outlives loss.

And on the day I stood at the reflecting pool as an adult, the sky above me was the same perfect blue as that morning long ago. But I was different. I was still standing.

Because even when the towers fell, I didn’t.

Mom dropped me off at school with a smile that didn’t reach her eyes — she had that nurse’s intuition, that quiet sense that something was coming. Before I walked through the school doors, she pulled me back into a hug. Long. Tight. The kind of hug you never forget.

She went to the hospital right after — just a few blocks from the towers.

I remember the sirens first. Then the way the teachers’ faces changed when the news came in. Some kids were pulled out of class in a rush. Others cried. I waited, small and confused, clutching my Spider-Man lunchbox.

No one came for me.

Hours passed. And then days. People spoke in hushed tones. "We can't reach them." "Still missing." "Maybe they got out."

But they didn’t.

For years, I lived in the in-between — between the child I had been and the weight of a world that had crumbled. I moved in with my aunt, who tried her best to hold together what was left of our little family. She taught me to pray. She held me when the nightmares came.

Every year on September 11th, I visited the memorial. I traced their names with my fingers — Miguel and Lucia — and tried to remember the way their laughter sounded in our old apartment. Sometimes I could. Sometimes I couldn’t.

Grief doesn’t go away. It just grows quieter. Becomes part of your shadow.

But here’s the thing: I didn’t break. I grew.

I kept their photos beside my bed. I studied hard. I lived for both of them — not just to make them proud, but to prove that love outlives loss.

And on the day I stood at the reflecting pool as an adult, the sky above me was the same perfect blue as that morning long ago. But I was different. I was still standing.

Because even when the towers fell, I didn’t.
